sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=31, col=19), LEFT, LEFT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 60, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Moving entity OUT of dark frame swaps center colors with entity at same row. Dark frame now empty. White entity at cols 12-14, charcoal at cols 18-20.
- Goal model: Maybe need both entities white-centered. Move charcoal entity through dark frame to make it white.
- Action model: Arrow keys move selected entity by 3 cells, blocked by purple bar.
- Plan: Move charcoal entity LEFT to cols 12-14, then DOWN into dark frame.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 59 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, something weird happened. The charcoal entity moved LEFT twice to cols 15-17, then LEFT again to cols 12-14... but wait, it went to cols 9-11! And when it moved DOWN, it went into a different position.
Let me look at the current state:
- Charcoal entity at cols 9-11, rows 30-32 (outside, to the left of dark frame)
- White entity at cols 15-17, rows 33-35 (inside dark frame area but shifted right)
The dark frame is at cols 11-15. The white entity is at cols 15-17, which is partially overlapping with the dark frame.
Wait, the segmentation shows:
- id=6: charcoal entity at cols 9-11, rows 30-32
- id=11: white entity at cols 15-17, rows 33-35
And the ascii:
- Row 30: gNNNwwwwwwwwwwwww (cols 9-11: NNN)
- Row 31: gNcNwwwwwwwwwwwww (cols 9-11: NcN, charcoal center)
- Row 32: gNNNccccwwwwwwwww (cols 9-11: NNN, cols 12-15: cccc)
- Row 33: gwwcwwwNNNwwwwwwg (cols 11-13: cwww, cols 15-17: NNN)
- Row 34: gwwcwwwNWNwwwwwwg (cols 11-13: cwww, cols 15-17: NWN, white center)
- Row 35: gwwcwwwNNNwwwwwwg (cols 11-13: cwww, cols 15-17: NNN)
- Row 36: gwwcccccwwwwwwwwg (cols 11-15: ccccc)
So the dark frame is at cols 11-15, rows 32-36. The white entity is at cols 15-17, which is to the RIGHT of the dark frame's main area. The dark frame interior (rows 33-35) has white cells at cols 12-14, and the entity is at cols 15-17.
I think what happened is:
1. Charcoal entity moved LEFT from cols 18-20 to cols 15-17 to cols 12-14 to cols 9-11 (3 moves of 3 cells each... but I only did 2 LEFTs)
Wait, I did LEFT, LEFT, DOWN. That's 2 LEFTs and 1 DOWN. The entity started at cols 18-20.
- After 1st LEFT: cols 15-17
- After 2nd LEFT: cols 12-14
- After DOWN: rows 33-35, cols 12-14
But the result shows the charcoal entity at cols 9-11, rows 30-32. That's not where I expected it.
And the white entity is at cols 15-17, rows 33-35. That's also not where I expected it.
I think what happened is:
1. The charcoal entity moved LEFT from cols 18-20 to cols 15-17.
2. The charcoal entity moved LEFT from cols 15-17 to cols 12-14. But there was a white entity at cols 12-14, rows 30-32. So they collided and something happened.
3. The DOWN move affected the white entity (which was selected? or the charcoal entity?).
Actually, I selected the charcoal entity at cols 18-20 with MOUSE. Then I moved it LEFT twice. After the first LEFT, it's at cols 15-17. After the second LEFT, it's at cols 12-14. But there's a white entity at cols 12-14, rows 30-32. So they collided.
When two entities collide, they might swap positions or center colors. Let me think...
Actually, looking at the result, the charcoal entity ended up at cols 9-11 (further left than expected), and the white entity ended up at cols 15-17, rows 33-35 (inside the dark frame area).
I think what happened is:
1. Charcoal entity moved LEFT to cols 15-17.
2. Charcoal entity moved LEFT to cols 12-14, but collided with white entity. The white entity was pushed LEFT to cols 9-11 and became charcoal. The charcoal entity took the white entity's place at cols 12-14 and became white.
3. DOWN move: the white entity (now at cols 12-14) moved DOWN into the dark frame, going to rows 33-35. But it ended up at cols 15-17, not 12-14.
Hmm, this is getting confusing. The entities seem to interact in complex ways when they collide or move through the dark frame.
Let me step back and think about what the goal might be. I've been trying many things without success. Let me consider a completely different hypothesis.
What if the goal is to have BOTH entities inside the dark frame? Or both at the same position?
Actually, let me look at the right panel again. The right dark frame is empty. What if the goal is to have the LEFT dark frame also empty, with both entities outside?
Currently:
- Charcoal entity at cols 9-11, rows 30-32 (outside)
- White entity at cols 15-17, rows 33-35 (inside dark frame area)
The dark frame interior has the white entity. To empty it, I need to move the white entity UP out of the dark frame.
Let me try: select the white entity and move it UP.
But wait, I've been going in circles. Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to match the right panel's configuration? The right panel has:
- Dark frame at rows 26-30, cols 44-48
- Empty interior
- No entities nearby
To match this, the left panel would need:
- Dark frame empty
- No entities nearby
But I can't destroy entities. So maybe the goal isn't to match the right panel.
Let me try yet another hypothesis. What if the goal is to have both entities with the SAME center color, regardless of position? Currently one is white and one is charcoal.
To make both white, I need to move the charcoal entity through the dark frame. But the dark frame currently has a white entity inside.
Let me try: move the white entity UP out of the dark frame (making the frame empty), then move the charcoal entity DOWN into the dark frame (making it white).
Actually, let me just try to get both entities to have white centers. The current state is:
- Charcoal entity at cols 9-11, rows 30-32
- White entity at cols 15-17, rows 33-35 (inside dark frame)
If I move the white entity UP, it exits the dark frame and the frame becomes empty. Then I can move the charcoal entity to the dark frame's column and move it DOWN.
Let me try this.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Charcoal entity at cols 9-11, white entity at cols 15-17 inside dark frame area. Dark frame partially occupied.
Goal model: Try to get both entities white-centered by moving white entity UP out of frame, then charcoal entity DOWN into frame.
Plan: Move white entity UP, then move charcoal entity to dark frame column and DOWN.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move white entity UP out of dark frame
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 16}])
action(['UP'])

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("After UP (rows 28-40, cols 8-25):")
for i in range(28, 41):
    print(f"  row {i}: {ascii_lines[i][8:25]}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nGreen entities:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        child_colors = [seg['nodes'][c]['color'] for c in n['children']]
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}, children={child_colors}")

print(f"\nResult: level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
